Let me say that again.<|16.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The good news of the incarnation is that God renounces pomp, pageantry, and power in order to bring our attention to those neighborhoods, communities, and nations others have written off.<|16.70|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> To write off stereotype or unnecessarily categorize whole peoples is to desecrate the word made flesh who comes to us not as a rule but as an invitation.<|12.04|> <|12.94|> Not as host but as guest.<|15.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus Christ.<|1.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A challenge of the days, months, and years ahead will be maintaining a center that is, in the words of theologian Ruby Sells, both tough-minded and tender-hearted.<|12.38|> <|13.92|> Toughness about systemic evil and sin and tenderness regarding every person and community impacted by it.<|22.78|> <|23.72|> Perpetrators and victims alike.<|26.26|> <|26.26|> Amen.<|27.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.28|> In these days, we hold the posture of Philip Close<|7.00|> <|7.00|> as we confront provincialism, racism, and xenophobia in all their forms,<|13.42|> <|13.54|> inviting the world to come and see.<|16.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Come and see this Jesus, this word made flesh.<|4.16|> <|4.56|> Come and see this person who shows us what God is like.<|8.28|> <|8.68|> Come and see who forgives us and expands our sense of meaning, belonging, and existence.<|15.50|> <|15.84|> Come and see who saw us.<|18.82|> <|19.82|> The one who saw us under the fig tree and loved us even then.<|25.76|> <|27.20|> Amen.<|27.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.44|><|endoftext|>
